title: Water sorption and diffusion coefficients of protons and water in PVDF-g-PSSA polymer electrolyte membranes
abstract:
Water Sorption and Diffusion Coefficients of Protons and
Water in PVDF-g-PSSA Polymer Electrolyte Membranes
SAMI HIETALA,1 SIRKKA LIISA MAUNU,1 FRANCISKA SUNDHOLM,1 TERO LEHTINEN,2 GO¨ RAN SUNDHOLM2
1 Laboratory of Polymer Chemistry, University of Helsinki, P.O. Box 55, FIN-00014 Helsinki, Finland
2 Laboratory of Physical Chemistry and Electrochemistry, Helsinki University of Technology,
P.O. Box 6100, FIN-02015 Helsinki, Finland
Received 19 January 1999; revised 15 June 1999; accepted 21 June 1999
ABSTRACT:
Water sorption properties, proton NMR spectra, and diffusion of water and
protons in poly(vinylidene ﬂuoride)-graft-polystyrene sulfonic acid (PVDF-g-PSSA)
polymer electrolyte membranes were studied. Sorption curves for the membranes with
different degrees of grafting in protonated and Na1 form were measured by equilibrat-
ing the membranes over saturated salt solutions. The membrane water content was
found to be sensitive to changes in relative humidity (RH). The water/sulfonic acid ratio
l for the protonated samples was around 2 at 20% RH and increased to l ; 30 at 100%.
Proton NMR, pulsed ﬁeld gradient proton NMR (PFG-NMR), and impedance measure-
ments were made on membranes with different l. In the proton NMR spectra only one
peak was found, originating from the water in the membrane. The chemical shift of the
peak was found to be dependent on the counterion and the water content. The water
self-diffusion coefﬁcients DH2O, measured by PFG-NMR, increased with degree of
grafting and water content of the membranes. The proton conductivity and the calcu-
lated proton mobility decreased more steeply than the DH2O with decreasing water
content. © 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. J Polym Sci B: Polym Phys 37: 2893–2900, 1999
Keywords:
polyelectrolyte membranes; water sorption; self-diffusion; proton conduc-
tivity; fuel cells
INTRODUCTION
Membranes with high proton conductivity are po-
tentially useful as separators and electrolytes in
electrochemical cells including fuel cells. Poly-
meric materials most commonly used are based
on poly(perﬂuorosulfonic acid), such as the Na-
ﬁon® series, that have suitable chemical and
physical properties for use in demanding electro-
chemical
applications.1–4
However,
since
the
price of these membranes is very high, other
membrane materials are being sought.
One way to produce new, less costly materials
is the functionalization of a preformed polymer
ﬁlm by radiation initiated grafting.5–13 We have
reported13–19 the preparation and the character-
ization of proton conducting membranes based on
poly(vinylidene ﬂuoride) (PVDF). The three-step
preparation procedure involves the electron beam
irradiation of the preformed PVDF matrix, graft-
ing to varying degrees with styrene and subse-
quent sulfonation. The membranes have been
characterized by several methods, including me-
chanical testing,15 swelling tests,13 differential
calorimetry,14,16 small angle and wide angle X-ray
diffraction14,16 (SAXS and WAXS, respectively),
impedance spectroscopy,13,17 thermogravimetric
measurements,18 and Raman spectroscopy.19
In a polymer electrolyte fuel cell, the cell per-
formance is strongly dependent on the water con-
tent and the proton conductivity of the mem-
Correspondence to: S. Hietala (E-mail: shietala@helsinki.ﬁ)
Journal of Polymer Science: Part B: Polymer Physics, Vol. 37, 2893–2900 (1999)
© 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
CCC 0887-6266/99/202893-08
2893
 brane. Although the mechanism of conduction
and the role of water are not known in detail, it is
clear that the conductivity is dependent on the
state of hydration of the membrane. In a water-
swollen membrane, the protons are solvated and
enabled to be transported through the membrane.
If the membrane is too dry, the water is associ-
ated with ionic sites only and does not facilitate
ion transport through the membrane, and thus
the conductivity decreases. On the other hand, it
must be considered that increased membrane wa-
ter content can lead to excessive swelling of the
assembly.11
Since the conductivity (and, therefore, the mo-
bility of protons) in the membrane is dependent
on the water content of the membrane, it is nec-
essary to understand how it is related to the mo-
bility of water in the membrane. This is accom-
plished by comparing the diffusion coefﬁcient of
protons DH1 and the diffusion coefﬁcient of water
in the membrane DH2O at different membrane
water contents. This can reveal information on
the conduction mechanism at different water con-
tents.
Pulsed ﬁeld gradient NMR (PFG-NMR) can ex-
tract the self-diffusion coefﬁcients of mobile 1H
species in the membrane.20 As the ratio of water
protons to acid protons is typically over 6 in the
membranes, the 1H signal arises mostly from the
water in the membrane and the diffusion coefﬁ-
cient is a close approximation of the water self-
diffusion coefﬁcient DH2O. This value can then be
compared with the proton diffusion coefﬁcient
DH1 estimated from conductivity data.
In this investigation we have made water sorp-
tion studies and used impedance spectroscopy,
proton NMR, and PFG-NMR spectroscopy to
characterize the materials.
EXPERIMENTAL
Materials
The
poly(vinylidene
ﬂuoride)-graft-polystyrene
sulfonic acid (PVDF-g-PSSA) membranes were
prepared by the three-step method described in
previous studies.13,15 The commercial PVDF ﬁlm
(Goodfellow, Cambridge, England, 80 mm), was
irradiated with electron beam, producing radical
sites that initiate grafting onto the PVDF chain.
The grafting of the membranes was carried out
immediately after irradiation in a 50/50 v/v mix-
ture of styrene (Fluka, Buchs, Switzerland) and
toluene (LabScan, Dublin, Ireland) (both of them
reagent grade, used as received) for different pe-
riods of time. The membranes were then ex-
tracted in a Soxhlet apparatus with chloroform
overnight and dried to constant mass. The degree
of grafting is deﬁned as
degree of grafting 5
m1 2 m0
m0
3 100%
(1)
where m0 is the original mass of the sample and
m1 the mass of the grafted sample. The mem-
branes were sulfonated by 0.5M chlorosulfonic
acid
(Merck,
Darmstadt,
Germany,
reagent
grade) in 1,2 dichloroethane (Merck, reagent
grade) for 24 h. Following the sulfonation, the
PVDF-g-PSSA membranes were washed ﬁrst
with dichloroethane and then with distilled water
and stored in plastic bags under ambient condi-
tions. Prior to investigation the membranes were
boiled for 1 h in distilled water.
Water Sorption Properties
To determine the water sorption properties, the
PVDF-g-PSSA membrane samples (;50 mg) were
subjected to relative humidities (RH) of 20–100%
at ambient temperature. The membranes were
stored for one week in a closed vessel over satu-
rated salt solutions and weighed. The dry weight
of the membranes was determined by drying the
samples overnight in vacuo at 70°C. The water
uptake was
water uptake 5
wwet 2 wdry
wdry
(2)
where wdry is the mass of the dried sample and
wwet the mass of the wet sample. The residual
water content of the dried membranes bound to
sulfonic acid groups in the membrane after drying
is believed to be less than 1 water per sulfonate
and has been discussed elsewhere.18
The dried PVDF-g-PSSA membranes were sub-
sequently subjected to different RH as described
and the water uptakes were measured. The so-
dium exchanged samples were prepared by keep-
ing these samples subsequently in a 0.1M NaOH
solution for several days. The water uptakes of
these Na1-exchanged samples was then mea-
sured. After this the samples were dried and the
dry weight of the Na1 form was measured. The
ion exchange capacity Q [meq/g] of the mem-

PVDF-g-PSSA membranes were washed ﬁrst

subjected to relative humidities (RH) of 20–100%

stored for one week in a closed vessel over satu-

where wdry is the mass of the dried sample and
wwet the mass of the wet sample. The residual
water content of the dried membranes bound to
sulfonic acid groups in the membrane after drying
is believed to be less than 1 water per sulfonate
and has been discussed elsewhere.18
The dried PVDF-g-PSSA membranes were sub-

and the water uptakes were measured. The so-
dium exchanged samples were prepared by keep-
ing these samples subsequently in a 0.1M NaOH
solution for several days. The water uptakes of
these Na1-exchanged samples was then mea-
sured. After this the samples were dried and the
dry weight of the Na1 form was measured. The
ion exchange capacity Q [meq/g] of the mem-
branes was determined titrimetrically using the
dry weight of the proton form of membranes as
presented in a previous study.16 The concentra-
tion of the sulfonic acid groups [meq/cm3] was
then calculated from the Q data after the dimen-

The water per sulfonic acid ratio l [n(H2O)/
2)] was calculated on the basis of the water
NMR and PFG-NMR Diffusion Measurements
For the NMR studies the boiled membrane sam-
ples were cut into strips and placed into standard
5-mm NMR tubes. The samples were equilibrated
for a minimum of three days to constant water
contents over saturated salt solutions prior to
The proton NMR spectra were recorded and
the relaxation time measurements were made at
room temperature with a Varian Gemini 2000
spectrometer operating at 200 MHz for protons.
An external dioxan (chemical shift d 5 3.7 ppm)
capillary was used as a chemical shift reference.
The diffusion experiments were carried out
with a Varian UNITYINOVA spectrometer operat-
ing at 300 MHz for protons, equipped with a gra-
dient probe by Varian Inc. The temperature was
set to be 22°C. In order to ascertain the applica-
bility of the PFG method for the diffusion studies,
the T1 relaxation times were measured by the
inversion recovery method. The relaxation times
varied within 50–170 ms for the samples humid-
iﬁed at 100% RH and decreased with decreasing
degree of grafting and water uptake. The relax-
ation times were long enough to make the diffu-
sion measurements possible for most of the sam-
ples. Only for the samples with the lowest degrees
of grafting or water content the relaxation times
were unacceptably short and the diffusion mea-
surement could not be carried out.
A stimulated echo sequence21 with time D 5 11
ms between the ﬁrst and last 90° pulse was ap-
plied. Sixteen spectra were recorded over linearly
incremented gradient strengths of 0–65 G/cm.
The diffusion coefﬁcient DH2O was calculated
from the slope of the signal intensity versus gra-
dient strength, obtained with a regression ﬁt of
the raw data. The instrument was calibrated by
measuring the diffusion coefﬁcient of a water
sample under the same experimental conditions.
Prior to conductivity measurements the mem-
branes were transformed into proton form by im-
mersing them in hot 0.5M sulfuric acid for 1 h
and, after rinsing, equilibrating them with water
vapor in a closed vessel for a minimum of 3 days.
The proton conductivity of the membranes was
determined by the impedance method using a fre-
quency range of 5–50 kHz. The measurements
were made in a solid two-electrode cell with a
heating jacket, the cell being connected to a So-
lartron 1170 frequency response analyzer and a
microcomputer. The membrane resistance was
obtained by extrapolating the linear part of the
spectrum to the real axis of the impedance plot.
The conductivity was calculated from the elec-
trode area of the cell (0.283 cm2) and the thick-
ness of the membrane, which was measured with
a micrometer. During the measurements the cell
was continuously purged with humidiﬁed nitro-
gen to maintain constant humidity in the sample.
The loss in weight of the sample during the mea-
surement was found to be below 5%. The temper-
ature of the cell was kept constant at 20°C by
circulating water from a thermostat.
The ion conductivity of the membranes depends
critically on their water content. We have previ-
ously reported measurements of the water uptake
of styrene grafted and sulfonated PVDF-based
membranes of the same type as in the present
work.16 It was found that the water uptake of the
membranes from liquid water corresponds to
around 40 molecules of water per sulfonic acid
group. In this study, the water uptakes were mea-
sured under atmospheres of different RH, since it
is believed that these conditions resemble the con-
The sorption data of the membranes equili-
brated at ambient temperature from water vapor
are presented in Figures 1 and 2. It is seen that
the water uptake increases with increasing RH as
well as with increasing degree of grafting (see
The water sorption data per sulfonic acid group
of the protonated samples, presented in Figure 2
as ﬁlled symbols, resemble the data reported for
proton forms of Naﬁon® 117 (l 5 14 at 100% RH
at 30°C20 and 25°C22) and Dowex® resins at
SORPTION/DIFFUSION IN PVDF-g-PSSA MEMBRANES
25°C22 (l ; 20 at 100% RH, depending on the
degree of crosslinking). All PVDF-g-PSSA sam-
ples also have a low water content region at 20–
75% RHs and a high water content region at
75–100% RHs, irrespective of the degree of graft-
ing. This type of sorption behavior with low water
contents at low RH and higher water contents at
high RH seems to be characteristic for systems
with sulfonic acid moieties, such as the Naﬁon
and Dowex resins. The differences in the sorption
isotherms between PVDF-g-PSSA, Naﬁon, and
Dowex arise from the differences in the hydrophi-
licity of the matrices, differences in the Q values,
and different abilities of the matrices to swell.
The effect of the drying procedure on the water
sorption in PVDF-g-PSSA membranes is shown in
Figure 2. The water content is similar at low
water contents but at high RH the membranes
dried at 70°C do not swell as much. Also the
liquid-state water uptake was smaller for these
dried membranes. Boiling in distilled water re-
stored the water uptake of the dried membranes
to the same level as in the boiled membranes.
This effect is thus possibly due to morphological
changes during drying at elevated temperatures.
These changes are reversed during boiling in
The Na1-exchanged samples of the PVDF-g-
PSSA membranes dried at 70°C show low values
for water uptake compared with the protonated
ones at all RHs. The decrease is a combined effect
of the larger ionic radius and lower hydration
afﬁnity of the Na1 ions compared with protons. A
Pushpa et al.22 shows that the values for Na1-
exchanged PVDF-g-PSSA samples fall between
the values for Li1- and Cs1-exchanged Dowex
samples. This is expected, since the chemical com-
position of the polystyrene sulfonic acid moiety is
The proton NMR spectra of protonated PVDF-g-
PSSA membrane samples are presented in Fig-
ures 3 and 4. Only one broad peak is observed for
the water/proton resonance at d 5 5–9 ppm
throughout the series of samples at different RHs.
The peak for liquid water at d 5 4.8 ppm is absent
from all of the spectra. This indicates that the
structure of water inside the membrane differs
from that of liquid water and that no large pock-
ets of bulk water are formed inside the mem-
brane. We have previously reported the absorp-
Water sorption in PVDF-g-PSSA mem-
branes. Filled symbols denote membranes in the H1
form, dotted symbols the same membranes equili-
brated after drying at 70°C, and open symbols mem-
branes in the Na1 form. Degrees of grafting of the
membranes: 32% (diamonds), 48% (squares), 60% (cir-
cles), and 73% (triangles), respectively.
Water uptake of the protonated PVDF-g-
PSSA membranes with degrees of grafting of * 18%, l
32%, f 48%, F 60%, and Œ 73%, respectively.
tion of water into these membranes in three dif-
ferent ways, the different water types termed
nonfreezable, freezable free, and freezable bound
water, based on thermal and spectroscopic mea-
surements.16 The existence of only one peak for
water in proton NMR spectra is somewhat con-
tradictory and is explained as follows. As the wa-
ter in the membranes resides associated with the
sulfonic acid groups, the protons from water and
the protons in ionic groups are exchanging at a
fast rate. The timescale of the NMR experiment is
large compared with this and the only signal ob-
served is the average signal of the different pro-
ton environments. Thus, the placement of the res-
onance line is the weighted average of the chem-
ical shift of acid protons, water, and hydronium
ions in equilibrium, as has previously been shown
by Dinius et al.23 As the water content of a mem-
brane decreases, the proton signal of the proton-
ated samples moves downﬁeld (see Fig. 4). The
major reason for this is the smaller water content
of the membrane and thereby increased relative
number of acid protons in the system.
The spectra for Na1-exchanged 100% RH
corded for comparison. Again only one peak cor-
responded to all the water in the membrane. The
peak linewidths were similar to those of the pro-
tonated samples, but the chemical shift of the
protons was now upﬁeld from the normal liquid
water resonance frequency at 4.8 ppm. As the
chemical shift is an average of the protonic spe-
cies, the absence of acid protons moves the peak
upﬁeld. Additionally, the Na1 ions might have an
effect on the water structure, which could lead to
a shift of the resonance line.
The results reported for water/resin suspen-
sions,23–26 where two different resonances existed
for bulklike and ionically associated water, are
explained by assuming that bulklike water out-
side the resin beads existed in these systems.
The chemical shift of pure water is dependent
on the degree of hydrogen bonding and varies
with temperature. The resonance line moves up-
ﬁeld when the hydrogen bonds in water are bro-
ken. The degree of hydrogen bonding in the mem-
brane is likely to be dependent on the water up-
take of the membrane. At low water contents the
water molecules have strong interactions with the
sulfonic acid groups and the hydrophobic polymer
matrix. It is likely that these interactions break
down the hydrogen bonds between water mole-
cules. This leads to an additional upﬁeld shift
contribution at lower membrane water contents.
The proton signal linewidth decreases with in-
creasing water uptake of the membrane sample.
This is explained by assuming the following. As
long as the membrane contains only a few water
molecules per sulfonic acid group, the motion of
this water is restricted by the surrounding poly-
mer matrix. This enhances the spin relaxation of
the water protons and results in increasing signal
Proton NMR spectra of PVDF-g-PSSA
sample with 60% degree of grafting at different RH.
Equilibrated at RH (a) 20% (l 5 2), (b) 65% (l 5 5), and
(c) 100% (l 5 30), respectively.
Proton NMR spectra of 100% RH equili-
brated PVDF-g-PSSA samples with different degrees of
grafting: (a) 18%, (b) 32%, (c) 48%, (d) 60%, and (e) 73%.
SORPTION/DIFFUSION IN PVDF-g-PSSA MEMBRANES
linewidth. As the membrane swells, the motion of
the water molecules is liberated, the relaxation
rate of the protons decreases, and the linewidth of
the signal is reduced. In addition, if a wide distri-
bution of water domain sizes exists in the mem-
brane, it will result in a large distribution of spin
relaxation times of the protons. This would lead to
Signal linewidths are generally decreasing
with increasing degree of grafting (see Fig. 3).
This is to be expected as the water uptake in-
creases with degree of grafting as well. However,
the increase in degree of grafting is accompanied
by other changes in the membrane structure. The
membrane inhomogeneity16,19 below a degree of
grafting of 30–40% causes differences in the local
concentration of the sulfonic acid groups in the
membrane. Raman measurements19 show that
variations of sulfonic acid concentration exist in
the membranes at low degrees of grafting. There
is the possibility that the sulfonic acid groups will
be hydrated differently, depending on their envi-
ronment. This would lead to signal broadening in
membranes with a low degree of grafting, due to
the shift of the resonance peak.
PFG-NMR Diffusion Measurements
The proton signal of the PVDF-g-PSSA samples is
a product of all rapidly exchanging 1H nuclei moi-
eties in the system, including complexes, H1, and
H2O. The samples measured by PFG-NMR in this
study had l ; 4, and the ratio of water protons to
acid protons is at least 8 : 1. The measured diffu-
sion coefﬁcient is therefore a close approximation
of the water self-diffusion coefﬁcient DH2O in the
sample, and this notation is used to cover all
these different forms. The PFG-NMR experiment
probes motions in the ms range and the measured
diffusion of molecules, in this case, occurs on a mm
scale. The measured value is, in essence, a mac-
roscopic diffusion coefﬁcient of the species.
The DH2O values of the PVDF-g-PSSA mem-
branes and Naﬁon 117 membrane20 are presented
in Figure 5. It is seen that the DH2O of the PVDF-
g-PSSA membranes increases with increasing l.
The change in DH2O is large at low l, but does not
increase very much over l 5 7–12 (see Fig. 5).
This large change at low water contents indicates
that the motion of the water molecules in the ﬁrst
hydration shell is hindered. With increasing l,
the motion of the water molecules is liberated.
The DH2O values increase with increasing degree
of grafting of the membranes and thus also with
increasing water uptake of the samples.
The behavior of the DH2O values with increas-
ing l, seen in Figure 5, is similar to that reported
for Naﬁon 11720 and for a sulfonated polyarylene
membrane.27 The measured DH2O values of the
PVDF-g-PSSA, however, differ from values re-
ported for Naﬁon 11720 and sulfonated poly-
arylene membrane.27 The extrapolation of the
Naﬁon 117 DH2O data at 30°C using a reported
activation energy of 16 kJ/mol28 gives a DH2O
5 4.9 3 1026 cm2/s at 22°C for a Naﬁon 117
sample equilibrated at 100% RH. This value is
smaller than the values measured for different
PVDF-g-PSSA membranes equilibrated at 100%
RH. On the other hand, the DH2O values reported
for a sulfonated polyarylene membrane are sub-
stantially smaller (;2 3 1026 cm2/s at 27°C, l
5 14) than the value reported for Naﬁon 117.
The differences in the DH2O values between
membranes can be explained by different water
contents and ion exchange capacities. The water
uptake of Naﬁon from gas phase,20 0.23 g/g, l
5 14, is signiﬁcantly lower than the water uptake
of the PVDF-g-PSSA membranes, in which the
lowest water uptake 0.35 g/g, was measured for a
membrane with a 32% degree of grafting, corre-
sponding to l 5 28. The sulfonated polyarylene
membrane on the other hand, with a water up-
take of 0.35 g/g (calculated from equivalent
weight and l data), l 5 14, has a lower DH2O than
the PVDF-g-PSSA membranes with the same wa-
DH2O of protonated PVDF-g-PSSA samples
as a function of l. Symbols denote membranes with
degrees of grafting l 32%, f 48%, F 60%, and Œ 73%.
Data for Naﬁon® extrapolated from Ref. 20 is denoted
ter content. The type of polymeric matrix and the
water uptake characteristics of the membranes
thus have a signiﬁcant inﬂuence on the values
Differences in the morphology of the systems
contribute to the difference in DH2O values as
well. The structure of Naﬁon is usually explained
by the cluster model29 resulting from molecular
rearrangement during the hydration of the mem-
brane. This structure has been described to be
optimal for conductivity and water localization.27
This water localization in the Naﬁon membrane
results in a higher DH2O compared with the ho-
Conductivity and Proton Mobility
The Nernst–Einstein equation for strong electro-
lytes was used to estimate the proton diffusion
coefﬁcients DH1 from the measured conductivity
data, even though the acid concentration exceeds
the generally accepted upper limit (0.1 mol/dm3)
of the range of applicability of the equation. The
DH1 and DH2O values for the PVDF-g-PSSA mem-
branes are compared in Figure 6. At high l, the
proton diffusion coefﬁcient is higher than the wa-
ter diffusion coefﬁcient. As the water content de-
creases, the proton diffusion coefﬁcient decreases
rapidly compared with the water diffusion coefﬁ-
cient. This behavior is different from the data
presented20 for the Naﬁon membrane, where the
diffusion coefﬁcients differed signiﬁcantly only at
high water contents and the DH1 remained
nearly the same as DH2O through different water
contents. The sulfonated polyarylene membrane
behaved similarly as the PVDF-g-PSSA mem-
These results are explained by assuming that
the morphology of the PVDF-g-PSSA membranes
is different from the clustered structure of Naﬁon,
because the acidic groups of these membranes are
not as free to rearrange themselves into clusters.
The water clusters in Naﬁon bind together even
at low water contents, enabling effective proton
transport.27 In contrast, in the PVDF-g-PSSA
membranes, the drying of the membrane makes
some of the acidic groups become isolated from
the conduction paths. When the acidic groups are
more dispersed, the water is dispersed among
them and fewer conduction paths are formed. The
passage of protons through the membrane is thus
more difﬁcult, resulting in a decrease in the mea-
sured conductivity and the calculated proton mo-
Since Naﬁon is perﬂuorinated and PVDF-g-
PSSA partially ﬂuorinated, the differences in the
chemistry of the polymers not only lead to differ-
ent dielectric properties of the matrices but also
can add to the observed differences in mobilities
Water sorption properties and diffusion of mobile
species in PVDF-g-PSSA electrolyte membranes
were studied. It was found that the membrane
water uptake depends on the degree of grafting,
the relative humidity of the environment, mem-
brane pretreatment, and type of counterion. The
water uptake increases with increasing degree of
grafting, although the water per sulfonic acid ra-
tio l remains almost constant. The increase in
water uptake was small up to 75% RH, but be-
came large at RHs over 75%. The boiled proton-
ated samples showed higher water contents than
the protonated samples dried at 70°C or the sam-
ples in Na1 form. Proton NMR of the membranes
showed only one type of water in the membrane at
all water contents, due to fast exchange of protons
in the different environments. The chemical shift
of water protons is dependent on the water con-
tent of the sample and moves downﬁeld with de-
creasing water content. Water diffusion coefﬁ-
cients DH2O measured by PFG-NMR were in the
range 1025–1026 cm2/s, and increased with in-
creasing degree of grafting and water uptake. The
change in DH2O was large at low water contents
Comparison of DH2O and DH1 as a function
of l of the PVDF-g-PSSA sample with a 60% degree of
SORPTION/DIFFUSION IN PVDF-g-PSSA MEMBRANES
but does not increase much for l 5 10–30. The
proton diffusion coefﬁcients calculated from the
conductivity data decreased drastically with de-
creasing water content. This effect was probably a
combined effect of lower water content leading to
lower DH2O and the isolation of the acidic groups
